title: The Ebon Spire
summary: Varth has promised a bigger payday at Vel Shara in the Unknown Regions. To get there, the crew needs ancient hyperspace charts. Denia points them to the Ebon Spire — Revan's forward base on the Forest Moon of Endor — where Jedi holocrons with navigational data should be stored. But the Dead Forest is strong with the Dark Side, and the Spire's defenses don't care that the war ended millennia ago.

marks.label: The Dead Forest
marks.desc: Navigate the toxic, Dark Side-saturated forest to reach the Spire. The moon resists you.

marks.label: Breaching the Spire
marks.desc: Find a way past the ancient defenses that still guard Revan's fortress.

marks.label: The Holocron Vault
marks.desc: Locate the Jedi holocrons containing the hyperspace charts to Vel Shara.

marks.label: Echoes of the Dark
marks.desc: Confront what the Dark Side shows you inside the Spire. It knows your fears.

marks.label: The Guardian
marks.desc: Face the ancient defender that still protects the Spire's secrets after millennia.

marks.label: The Way Forward
marks.desc: Secure the navigational data and escape the Spire before it seals you in.

marks.label: The Crucible
marks.desc: The plan catastrophically fails. Improvise under fire to survive.

gearNotes.reason: The Dead Forest has toxic spore clouds from the petrified vegetation. Without respiratory protection, characters take environmental penalties during the trek.
The Ebon Spire's interior is pitch black. Ancient lighting systems failed centuries ago. Without light sources, all checks inside the Spire are upgraded in difficulty.
The Descent into the Spire involves vertical shafts and collapsed stairways. Without rope, certain routes are impassable.
Multiple combat encounters and the Dark Echoes gauntlet both deal wounds. No medical facilities on the Forest Moon.
Scouting the Dead Forest perimeter and locating the Spire entrance from distance. The Ewoks can point you in the right direction, but the canopy is dense.
Imperial probe droids patrol the Endor system. Avoiding detection on approach keeps the crew off Draco's radar.
The Dark Echoes psychological gauntlet can inflict strain. Stim packs help recover between encounters in the Spire.
Extended wilderness trek through the Forest Moon. Navigation, shelter, and foraging between the Ewok village and the Dead Forest.

scenes.title: The Descent and the Briefing
scenes.subtitle: Planetfall on the Forest Moon
scenes.readAloud:
The Banshee punches through a canopy so dense it feels like sinking into a green ocean, its landing struts settling heavily into the damp, decaying mulch of a rare clearing. The scale of Endor is immediately oppressive. The trunks of the ancient trees are as wide as starships, their upper reaches entirely obscuring the sky, plunging the forest floor into a perpetual, emerald twilight. The air is heavy, smelling of crushed ferns, ozone, and rampant, unchecked life. The undergrowth presses in from every direction — a wall of thorned vines, luminescent fungi, and fern fronds taller than a Wookiee.

The Banshee's sensors are already struggling. The dense biomass and mineral-rich soil scatter readings like static. Beyond the clearing, the forest is an impenetrable wall of shadow and green light.
scenes.gmNotes:
This scene establishes tone and isolates the heroes. Varth stays aboard with Maya and Denia — his stated reason is tactical overwatch, but his real goal is keeping Denia (his primary asset for opening the holocrons) safe from the Dark Side energies of the Spire. He is risking the heroes' lives to fetch his prize while he remains completely safe.

Let the players feel the scale and hostility of the environment. This is not a civilized world. The forest is alive, indifferent, and enormous.

npcs.name: Admiral Varth
npcs.notes: Stays aboard the Banshee. Offers tactical comms support from the ship. Immaculately composed against the backdrop of untamed wilderness.
npcs.behavior: Charming, easy confidence. Adjusting his cuffs at the top of the ramp while the jungle presses in. The picture of a man who has someone else do the dangerous work.
dialogue: "Alright, Kids. Denia gave us the radius, but it's just a sea of interference out there. Maya and I will keep the ship warm and monitor your vitals from the chair. Try not to step in anything that wants to eat you."
"The Spire should be roughly two days' march northeast, based on Denia's projections. Watch your heading — the canopy will kill your nav signals fast."
npcs.intel: Varth is isolating Denia from the dark energies of the Spire while risking the heroes to retrieve the holocrons. His tactical overwatch is genuine but secondary to his real agenda.

npcs.name: Master Denia
npcs.notes: Resting in the med-bay. Provided the coordinates and radius for the Ebon Spire's location. Too weakened from her captivity to accompany the crew.
npcs.behavior: Quiet, contemplative. Senses the Dark Side presence but trusts the heroes.
npcs.intel: Denia pointed the crew to the Ebon Spire. Varth manipulated her into 'suggesting' it — he already knew about it.

npcEffects.fleeting: Designate one visible target. Next ally attack vs target gains [Optimized].
npcEffects.masterful: Designate one visible target. Next two ally attacks vs target gain [Optimized].
npcEffects.legendary: Designate one visible target. All ally attacks vs target gain [Optimized] Tactical.

passive.name: Aura of Resolve
passive.description: Allies within 2 zones gain +1 Defense in Support's chosen arena.

gambit.name: Smoke Cover
gambit.description: On personal attack: one ally in zone gains [Cover 1] Tactical.

gambits.name: Smoke Cover
gambits.description: On personal attack: one ally in zone gains [Cover 1] Tactical.

gambits.name: Marking Round
gambits.description: On personal attack: target gains [Marked] Tactical.

exploit.name: Overwatch Support
exploit.trigger: An ally in an adjacent zone is attacked.
exploit.description: Immediately attack the attacker at -2 Power if in weapon range.

narrativeLinks.note: The crew sets out into the forest on foot. The journey to the Ewok encounter begins.

pacing.openingBeat: The Banshee lands in a rare clearing. The oppressive scale of Endor hits immediately.
pacing.risingAction: Varth delivers the briefing from the top of the ramp. The crew gears up and enters the forest.
pacing.climax: The forest swallows them. Comms already crackle with interference. They are on their own.
pacing.resolution: The march begins. The heroes push into the green labyrinth.

scenes.title: First Blood
scenes.subtitle: The Forest Bites Back
scenes.readAloud:
The journey is profoundly disorienting. Every fern-choked ravine mirrors the last, the sheer density of the woods wearing down the mind and the body. Hours blur together under the unchanging emerald twilight.

Suddenly, the underbrush explodes. A lone Boar-wolf — a massive, tusked predator of matted fur and pure muscle — charges from the ferns, jaws wide, eyes wild with territorial fury. It is on the crew before anyone can shout a warning.
scenes.gmNotes:
A quick, brutal skirmish that shatters the silence and serves as a violent reminder: out here on the fringe, the heroes are no longer at the top of the food chain. This is a single predator — dangerous but manageable. It establishes the wildlife threat level before the Ewok ambush escalates things.

Keep it fast. The Boar-wolf fights to kill or be killed — no retreat behavior.

npcs.name: Boar-wolf
npcs.notes: Massive tusked predator. Matted fur, pure muscle. Territorial and aggressive. Charges without warning.
npcs.behavior: Pure aggression. Targets the nearest threat. No retreat — fights to the death.
npcs.intel: A lone territorial male. The pack encounter in Part 2 will be far worse.

narrativeLinks.note: After the Boar-wolf, the crew presses deeper — and the true masters of the forest reveal themselves.

pacing.openingBeat: Disorienting march through identical terrain. Fatigue setting in.
pacing.climax: Quick, brutal combat. The forest draws first blood.
pacing.resolution: The predator is down. The crew catches their breath and pushes on.

scenes.title: The Canopy Ambush
scenes.subtitle: Ewok First Contact
scenes.readAloud:
An hour later, the true masters of the forest reveal themselves.

The canopy rains crude spears and bolas. Figures drop from the branches above with silent, practiced lethal intent — small, furred, wrapped in animal pelts, aiming poisoned darts at the vulnerable joints in the crew's armor. They are not the cuddly creatures of Republic fables. They are pragmatic, primitive survivalists who have learned that anything walking their forest uninvited is either food or a threat.

The crew is surrounded.
scenes.gmNotes:
The Ewoks are NOT comic relief. They are effective, coordinated, and willing to kill. The ambush should feel genuinely dangerous — the Ewoks have absolute home-field advantage in the canopy.

This scene is the setup for the Parley or Pyre decision. The heroes need to realize that fighting their way through will work but at a cost — and that lowering their weapons opens a different path entirely.

npcs.name: Ewok War Party
npcs.notes: Coordinated ambush from canopy positions. Stone axes, crude spears, bolas, poisoned darts. Absolutely lethal in their home terrain.
npcs.behavior: Disciplined pack tactics. Drop from above, pin movement with bolas, target gaps in armor with poison darts. They will retreat into the canopy if the heroes show overwhelming force — but they will return with more.
npcs.intel: The Ewoks control this forest. Fighting through them is possible but wasteful. Diplomacy opens the path to the Storyteller and critical intelligence about the Dead Forest.

narrativeLinks.note: The crew must decide: fight through the Ewoks or try to make peace.

pacing.openingBeat: The canopy erupts. Spears, bolas, poisoned darts rain down.
pacing.risingAction: The crew is surrounded. The Ewoks have the high ground and numbers.
pacing.climax: The moment of decision — do they escalate or de-escalate?
pacing.resolution: Transition to Scene 4 based on the crew's choice.

scenes.title: Parley or Pyre
scenes.subtitle: The Meaningful Decision
scenes.readAloud:
The Ewoks hold their fire — barely. Small dark eyes watch from the branches above, bowstrings taut, stone-tipped spears leveled. The forest holds its breath.

The crew faces a choice of conscience. They are on a ticking clock for their payday, and clearing this primitive obstacle with overwhelming firepower would be swift. But to slaughter the forest's natives is to invite the wrath of the woods and stain their own spirits.
scenes.gmNotes:
This is the first major moral decision of the adventure. The choice has real consequences:

PARLEY: If the heroes lower their blasters and attempt to bridge the language barrier through patience, empathy, or a Force-projection of calm, the Ewoks respond to sincerity. They are escorted to the village (Scene 5). They gain guides, intelligence about the Dead Forest, and the Storyteller's warning.

PYRE: If the heroes fight through, the Ewoks scatter but the forest becomes hostile. No guides, no intelligence, no Storyteller scene. The trek to the Dead Forest is longer, harder, and the crew arrives depleted. The Ewok totem blessing in Part 2 is unavailable.

Either way, the adventure continues — but the cost of violence echoes forward.

decisions.choice: Parley — Lower weapons and attempt diplomacy with the Ewoks
decisions.consequence: The Ewoks escort the crew to the village. They meet Chief Chirpa, the Storyteller, and gain guides and intelligence about the Dead Forest. The Ewok blessing becomes available in Part 2.

decisions.choice: Pyre — Fight through the Ewok ambush with overwhelming force
decisions.consequence: The Ewoks scatter but the forest turns hostile. No village scene, no guides, no Storyteller intelligence. The trek to the Dead Forest is longer and harder. The crew arrives at the Spire depleted and without the Ewok blessing.

narrativeLinks.note: If peace is brokered, the crew is escorted to the Ewok village and meets the Storyteller.
If the crew fights through, skip the village and proceed directly to Part 2 — the trek to the Dead Forest without guides.

pacing.openingBeat: Tense standoff. Ewoks hold fire. The crew decides.
pacing.risingAction: The language barrier. Patience, empathy, or Force calm required.
pacing.climax: The moment the Ewoks either accept the strangers or scatter under fire.

scenes.title: The Storyteller's Fire
scenes.subtitle: Chief Chirpa's Village
scenes.readAloud:
The Ewok warriors escort the crew high into the canopy, climbing rope ladders and crossing swaying bridges woven from living vines. The village is suspended among the ancient branches — a sprawling network of huts, platforms, and communal fires, alive with the bustle of a small, thriving civilization hidden above the forest floor.

Around a roaring fire at the village center, Chief Chirpa watches the strangers with the steady, unreadable gaze of a leader who has survived everything the forest has thrown at his people. Beside him, a young, energetic Ewok named Paploo chatters excitedly, clearly fascinated by the outsiders and their strange equipment.

Then the Storyteller approaches. Ancient, hunched, wrapped in feathers and bone charms. Through pantomime, guttural clicks, and ash drawings on a stretched hide, the Storyteller speaks of the forest's dangers. Then, they draw a colossal tree, rubbing charcoal over it until it is pitch black, mimicking profound dread.

The Ewoks fear this place. It is a dead zone where the forest goes quiet. The Storyteller's warning is unmistakable: what lives in the black tree is older than the Ewoks, older than the forest, and it does not sleep — it waits.
scenes.gmNotes:
This scene only triggers if the heroes chose diplomacy in Scene 4.

Chief Chirpa is the authority — stoic, watchful, slow to trust but honorable. Paploo is the comic relief and the bridge — curious, eager, the Ewok most willing to engage with the outsiders. The Storyteller delivers the critical intelligence about the Dead Forest and the Ebon Spire.

The Storyteller's warning should land with weight. The Ewoks are apex survivors. Whatever is in the Dead Forest frightens them enough that they have mapped it as forbidden ground for generations.

Chirpa agrees to provide ten Ewok scouts as guides to the boundary of the Dead Forest — but they will not enter. This sets up the guide dynamic for Part 2.

npcs.name: Chief Chirpa
npcs.notes: Leader of the village. Stoic, watchful, a survivor. Commands absolute respect from his people.
npcs.behavior: Observes before acting. Communicates through gestures and the village translator (pantomime and context). Slow to trust but keeps his word.
npcs.intel: Chirpa agrees to provide guides to the boundary of the Dead Forest. His people will not enter the dead zone. He has seen what happens to those who do.

npcs.name: Paploo
npcs.notes: Young, energetic, fascinated by the outsiders' technology. The bridge between cultures.
npcs.behavior: Curious and enthusiastic. Pokes at equipment, mimics the heroes. Genuinely helpful despite the communication gap.
npcs.intel: Paploo volunteers as lead scout for the Part 2 trek. He is brave but terrified of the Dead Forest.

npcs.name: The Storyteller
npcs.notes: Ancient, hunched, wrapped in feathers and bone charms. The village's living memory.
npcs.behavior: Performs rather than speaks. Uses fire, charcoal, pantomime, and ritual to communicate. The warning about the black tree is delivered as a solemn, fearful performance.
npcs.intel: The Storyteller's warning: the Dead Forest is forbidden ground. The black tree is older than the Ewoks, older than the forest. It does not sleep — it waits. This is the crew's first real intelligence about the Dark Side Nexus.

narrativeLinks.note: The crew departs the village at dawn with ten Ewok scouts as guides.

pacing.openingBeat: Ascent into the canopy village. The scale and sophistication of Ewok civilization.
pacing.risingAction: Introduction to Chirpa and Paploo. Cultural exchange around the fire.
pacing.climax: The Storyteller's warning. The charcoal drawing of the black tree. Unmistakable dread.
pacing.resolution: Chirpa agrees to provide scouts. The crew rests and prepares to depart at dawn.

scenes.title: The Creeping Quiet
scenes.subtitle: Into the Dead Zone
scenes.readAloud:
The crew departs the village at dawn, escorted by ten Ewok scouts wielding crude stone axes. Paploo leads the formation, his earlier enthusiasm replaced by grim focus.

As they march deeper into the woods, the vibrant, deafening noise of the living forest begins to mute. The birdsong fades. The insect drone dies. The vibrant green ferns turn brittle; the damp earth gives way to gray, powdery dust. The air grows unnaturally cold.

The wildlife hasn't just vanished. It is actively repelled by the corruption bleeding from the earth ahead. The Ewok scouts grow increasingly agitated, their dark eyes scanning the dead ferns with the practiced wariness of prey animals entering predator territory.
scenes.gmNotes:
This scene is atmospheric setup. The transition from living forest to dead zone should be gradual and deeply unsettling. Use sensory details — the silence is the most powerful tool here. Players used to the constant noise of the living forest should feel the absence like a physical weight.

If the crew fought through the Ewoks (skipped Scene 5), they have no guides. The trek is longer, more disorienting, and they arrive at the Dead Forest boundary depleted. Adjust the read-aloud to remove the Ewok escort references.

The Ewok scouts are visibly terrified but honor their commitment to Chirpa.

npcs.name: Ewok Scouts
npcs.notes: Provided by Chief Chirpa. Skilled forest navigators but terrified of the Dead Forest. They will not cross the boundary.
npcs.behavior: Professional but increasingly frightened. Tight formation, constant scanning, whispered communication. Paploo stays at the front but his hands tremble.
npcs.intel: The scouts know the fastest path to the boundary. Without them, the crew would lose hours to the disorienting terrain.

narrativeLinks.note: Just before the dead zone boundary, the forest delivers one final test of character.

pacing.openingBeat: Dawn departure. Ewok escort in formation. The march begins.
pacing.risingAction: Gradual environmental decay. Sound dies. Color drains. Temperature drops.
pacing.climax: The moment the crew realizes the forest is actively repelling life from ahead.
pacing.resolution: The boundary is close. The scouts are terrified. Something is coming.

scenes.title: The Test of Conscience
scenes.subtitle: Boar-wolf Pack Attack
scenes.readAloud:
Just before the dead zone, the brush erupts. It is not a single Boar-wolf this time, but a starving pack — driven mad by the encroaching dark or sheer hunger. The beasts bypass the heavily armored heroes entirely. Instead, they lunge with slavering jaws directly at the vulnerable, lightly armed Ewok guides.

Over the comms, Varth's voice cuts through the screams of the pack, cold and utterly pragmatic.

"Don't waste your ammo on the locals, Ace. We aren't getting paid to be park rangers. Secure the perimeter and move on."

It is a chilling flash of the monster beneath the charming employer — a demand to discard empathy for efficiency.
scenes.gmNotes:
This is the defining moral moment of Part 2. The wolves specifically target the Ewoks — the weakest, most vulnerable targets. The heroes must choose between their mission (conserve resources for the Spire) and their conscience (protect the people who guided them here).

Varth's line is critical. It should land like ice water. This is the third red flag after 'Leave her — she's a liability' (Denia, Adv2) and the Raden execution (Adv2). Players tracking his behavior will feel the pattern crystallizing.

If the crew skipped the village (Pyre path), there are no Ewok guides to protect — the pack attacks the crew directly. The moral dimension is removed and it becomes a straight combat encounter. This is a consequence of the Pyre choice: they traded empathy for efficiency in Scene 4, and now there's no one left to protect.

SAVE THE EWOKS: The crew takes injuries but the guides survive (some may die regardless). The lead Ewok gives them a carved wooden totem at the boundary — a blessing.

LET THEM DIE: The crew conserves strength but walks over the bodies of those who trusted them. No totem, no blessing. Carrion birds gather in the canopy.

npcs.name: Boar-wolf Pack
npcs.notes: Driven mad by the Dark Side corruption. Starving, desperate, bypassing armored targets for vulnerable prey.
npcs.behavior: Frenzied pack tactics. Target the Ewoks exclusively unless forced to engage the heroes. Will not retreat — too starved and maddened.
npcs.intel: The pack's behavior is unnatural. The Dark Side corruption is driving wildlife to desperation at the boundary of the dead zone.

decisions.choice: Save the Ewoks — throw themselves into the jaws of the pack to protect their guides
decisions.consequence: The crew takes injuries but saves most of the scouts. At the boundary, the lead Ewok presses a carved wooden totem into a hero's hand — a quiet blessing from a harsh world. The crew has allies in the forest.

decisions.choice: Let them die — conserve strength for the Spire and prioritize the mission
decisions.consequence: The Ewok guides are slaughtered. The crew steps over the bodies of those who trusted them. No totem, no blessing. Only the sound of carrion birds gathering in the canopy.

narrativeLinks.note: The fight ends. The surviving Ewoks (if any) refuse to go further. The boundary awaits.

pacing.openingBeat: The brush erupts. A starving pack of Boar-wolves targets the Ewok guides.
pacing.risingAction: Varth's cold command over comms. The crew must decide in the chaos.
pacing.climax: The choice — bleed for the Ewoks or walk away.
pacing.resolution: The aftermath. Blood soaking into gray dust. The cost of either choice.

scenes.title: The Boundary
scenes.subtitle: The Living Forest Ends
scenes.readAloud:
The fight ends, the blood soaking into the gray dust. The surviving Ewoks are terrified, trembling not just from the wolves but from what lies ahead. They refuse to take another step.

Before the crew is a sharp, unnatural line where the living forest abruptly ends and the petrified dead zone begins. On one side: green, breathing, alive. On the other: gray, silent, and utterly still. The transition is not gradual. It is a wound in the earth, drawn with geometric precision.

The Ewoks will go no further. They have honored their word to Chirpa. What lies beyond is not their world.
scenes.gmNotes:
The boundary is the point of no return. Once the crew crosses, they are entirely alone — no guides, no Varth on comms (the Nexus will sever the link in Part 3), no support.

TOTEM MOMENT: If the crew saved the Ewoks, the lead scout presses a small, carved wooden totem into a hero's hand with a solemn bow before melting back into the living forest. This is a quiet, earned moment of grace. The totem may have mechanical significance later (resistance to Dark Side effects in the Spire) or it may simply be a narrative touchstone — a reminder that someone out there is grateful.

NO TOTEM: If the crew let the Ewoks die, there is only silence and carrion birds. The bridge is burned. They step over the bodies and cross alone.

Either way, the heroes are now entirely on their own.

narrativeLinks.note: The crew crosses the boundary into the dead zone and approaches the Ebon Spire.

pacing.openingBeat: The aftermath of the fight. Blood in the dust. The Ewoks refuse to continue.
pacing.risingAction: The boundary — a geometric wound where life ends and death begins.
pacing.climax: The totem moment (if earned). A quiet beat of grace before the dark.
pacing.resolution: The crew crosses the line. They are alone.

scenes.title: The Black Tree and the Door
scenes.subtitle: The Ebon Spire Revealed
scenes.readAloud:
Stepping across the boundary, the crew enters a massive clearing of withered, dead vegetation. In the center stands the Ebon Spire.

It is not a tower built of stone, but a colossal, ancient tree that has been entirely petrified into black, obsidian-like rock. Its trunk is wider than a corvette, its dead branches clawing at a sky choked with gray, unmoving clouds. The Dark Side radiates from it like heat from a reactor core — a physical pressure against the chest, a whisper at the edge of hearing.

At the base of its massive trunk is a seamless, carved stone door, perfectly integrated into the petrified wood. But a chilling detail awaits: the thick, gray moss that should have covered the door for millennia has been torn away. There are fresh scrape marks on the stone. Someone else has been here recently.

The door has no handles or latches. Opening it requires a deep attunement to the Force — sensing dormant tumblers deep within the stone-wood and telekinetically grinding them open. Without the Force, it requires masterful, risky skullduggery — finding micro-fissures in the ancient bark to trigger pressure plates without bringing a dead-fall trap down upon their heads.

As the heavy stone finally grinds inward, exhaling a breath of absolute darkness, crypt-dust, and ozone into the crew's faces, the comms crackle. Varth's voice returns, warm, grinning, and encouraging — completely erasing the coldness from ten minutes ago.

"There it is. Good work, Boss. I knew you could handle the wildlife. Grab those charts and let's get off this rock. Drinks are on me when we hit Vel Shara."
scenes.gmNotes:
The Ebon Spire reveal should be awe-inspiring and deeply wrong. A tree the size of a capital ship, turned to black glass by millennia of Dark Side corruption. It should feel ancient, powerful, and malevolent.

THE SCRAPE MARKS: This is the first sign that Inquisitor Draco has been here. Fresh damage on a door that hasn't been touched in centuries. Don't explain it — let the players draw their own conclusions. The payoff comes in Part 3, Scene 1.

THE DOOR PUZZLE: This is a skill challenge, not a combat encounter. Force-sensitive characters can sense the mechanism. Non-Force characters need slicing/mechanics skills and accept higher risk (dead-fall traps). Either approach works.

VARTH'S WHIPLASH: His warm, charming return after the cold 'don't waste ammo on the locals' line is deliberate. He is a skilled manipulator who knows when to apply warmth and when to apply pressure. Players should feel the tonal whiplash — charm after cruelty. This is how abusers operate.

The last line of comms before the Nexus severs contact in Part 3.

narrativeLinks.note: The door opens. The dark awaits. Part 3 begins.

pacing.openingBeat: The clearing. The Ebon Spire revealed — a petrified titan of black glass.
pacing.risingAction: The scrape marks. Someone was here before them. The door puzzle.
pacing.climax: The door grinds open. Absolute darkness exhales.
pacing.resolution: Varth's warm, grinning comm — the last voice they'll hear before the dark takes them.

scenes.title: The Shattered Seal
scenes.subtitle: The Broken Survivor
scenes.readAloud:
The Endor twilight vanishes the moment the crew slips through the breached door, plunging them into absolute, suffocating darkness. The air is ancient — cold, still, and heavy with the mineral taste of stone that hasn't breathed in millennia.

In a ruined antechamber just inside the threshold, illuminated only by the cutting beams of their glowrods, they find the toll the Inquisitor paid to open the door.

Curled on the freezing stone floor is a man. Emaciated. Charred. His robes are the tattered remnants of Jedi vestments, scorched and crusted with old blood. He is trapped inside a pulsating energy cage — a Universal Energy Cage of Imperial design, its harsh blue light the only illumination in the antechamber. His eyes are open but vacant, staring at nothing.

This is Master Thorla. A survivor of Order 66. Draco dragged him here, tortured him until his mind broke open, and used his shattered attunement to crack the ancient Force-lock on the door. Then he left him here, discarded like a spent power cell, caged in the dark.
scenes.readAloudPart1PauseNote: Let this image sit. A broken Jedi, caged and abandoned in the dark by the Empire. Give the players a moment to process before presenting the decision.
scenes.gmNotes:
This scene is the emotional anchor of Part 3. Thorla is a mirror for Denia — another Jedi survivor used and discarded by someone more powerful. The parallel to Varth's treatment of Denia ('Leave her — she's a liability') should resonate.

THORLA'S STATE: He is physically alive but spiritually destroyed. Order 66 shattered his faith. Draco's torture finished the job. He weeps, refuses to help, tells them the Jedi are dead, and begs to be left alone. He is not a quest-giver — he is a broken human being.

THE CAGE: Imperial Universal Energy Cage. Rigged to deliver brutal shocks if tampered with. Disabling it requires technical skill and time — both of which are precious resources in the Spire.

COMMS SEVERED: The sheer density of the Dark Side Nexus bleeding from the open Spire completely severs the comm-link. Varth's charming confidence fractures into a hiss of static before dying completely. The heroes are in the dark, entirely alone.

SAVE THORLA: Costs time and resources. Thorla is too broken to fight but can provide fragmentary intelligence about Draco's movements inside the Spire. If brought out alive, he becomes a potential holocron interpreter in the Adventure 5 interlude (alternate to Denia).

LEAVE THORLA: Faster, cheaper. The crew steps over another discarded person — repeating the same cold calculus Varth demands of them. The parallel should sting.

npcs.name: Master Thorla
npcs.notes: Survivor of Order 66. Tortured by Draco to crack the Spire's Force-lock. Caged and abandoned. Physically alive, spiritually destroyed.
npcs.behavior: Weeps. Flinches from light. Refuses help initially. Begs to be left alone. 'The Jedi are dead. There's nothing left.' If coaxed back, speaks in fragmented whispers about Draco — how many troopers he has, that he was looking for something specific.
dialogue: "Leave me. Please. There's nothing left."
"The Jedi are dead. We died with the Republic. What's one more corpse in the dark?"
"He... the Inquisitor. He needed me to open the door. My hands. My mind. He... took what he needed and left me here."
"There were soldiers with him. Dark armor. Heavy weapons. He went down. Into the deep. He was looking for something... red."
npcs.intel: Thorla can confirm Draco's presence, his Dark Trooper escort, and that he descended deeper into the Spire seeking something specific (the Sith Holocron). If rescued, Thorla becomes a potential holocron interpreter for the Adventure 5 interlude.

decisions.choice: Compassion — Sacrifice time and resources to free Thorla from the cage and coax him back from the edge
decisions.consequence: The crew gains fragmentary intelligence about Draco's forces and movements. Thorla is too broken to fight but can be evacuated. He becomes a potential holocron interpreter in the Adventure 5 interlude. The parallel to Denia's rescue is honored.

decisions.choice: The Clock — Step over the discarded Jedi and press deeper into the Spire, prioritizing the mission
decisions.consequence: Faster progress but the crew repeats the cold calculus Varth demands of them. No intelligence about Draco's disposition. Thorla is abandoned in the dark — another life discarded for the payday.

narrativeLinks.note: The crew descends deeper into the Spire, following Draco's trail of destruction.

pacing.openingBeat: Absolute darkness. The comms die. The crew is alone.
pacing.risingAction: The energy cage. The broken Jedi. The horror of what Draco did to get through the door.
pacing.climax: The decision — compassion or the clock.
pacing.resolution: The crew moves deeper. Draco's trail leads down.

scenes.title: The Ruined Archives
scenes.subtitle: The Dead and the Mirror
scenes.readAloud:
Following Draco's trail of destruction, the crew descends into what used to be Revan's ancient library. It has been pulverized. Shattered obsidian shelves and the burnt ashes of ancient texts cover the floor like black snow. Scattered among the wreckage are the decomposing, mummified corpses of looters and cultists who found this place centuries ago and wiped each other out in the dark.

Draco has clearly just moved through here, cleaving ancient statues and obliterating what he didn't need to ensure nothing of value remained behind him. The holocron vault — a cavity in the far wall — has been torn open. Whatever was stored in the secondary alcoves has been shattered or taken.

But among the dead, clutched in the skeletal fingers of a centuries-old corpse, something glints. A data-chip, cracked but intact, containing a fragmentary log written in Old Galactic Basic. Its author warns of the 'hall of echoes' deeper in the Spire — a place where the dark feeds on memory and turns it against the intruder.
scenes.gmNotes:
This scene is atmospheric and provides two things: intelligence (the fragmentary log warning about the Hall of Mirrors ahead) and thematic resonance (the dead cultists as a mirror for the crew's own greed).

THE DEAD CULTISTS: These are people who came here centuries ago seeking Revan's power. They found it, fought over it, and killed each other in the dark. Their mummified corpses are a silent warning — a mirror held up to the terminal end of obsession. The crew is looking at what greed leaves behind when the galaxy stops watching.

THE DATA-CHIP: A fragmentary warning about the Hall of Mirrors (Scene 3). If the crew reads it, they have forewarning about what's ahead. If they skip it, the Hall hits them cold.

DRACO'S TRAIL: Deliberate, methodical destruction. He took what he wanted and scorched the rest. This is the Imperial way — extract value, destroy the remainder.

narrativeLinks.note: The fragmentary log warns of the 'hall of echoes' deeper in the Spire.

pacing.openingBeat: The ruined library. Shattered shelves, burnt texts, mummified corpses.
pacing.risingAction: Draco's methodical destruction. The empty holocron vault. He was thorough.
pacing.climax: The data-chip discovery. A centuries-old warning about what lies deeper.
pacing.resolution: The crew absorbs the warning and descends further.

scenes.title: The Hall of Mirrors
scenes.subtitle: The Dark Side Attacks
scenes.readAloud:
As the crew descends deeper into Revan's deliberate masonry, the Dark Side Nexus actively attacks their minds. An unnatural, creeping cold envelops them. Frost blooms on armor plating. Breath comes in visible clouds.

Suddenly, the glowrod beams isolate them. A hero looks back and realizes they are separated from the group. Solid walls of dark energy seem to have materialized from nowhere. They step into a room and come face-to-face with a phantom from their past — a former enemy, the bleeding ghosts of refugees, or a betrayed friend.

The nightmare knows their name. It speaks with voices that should be impossible. It reaches into the worst corners of their memory and drags out the things they buried.
scenes.gmNotes:
This is the psychological gauntlet. The Dark Side Nexus weaponizes the heroes' own pasts against them. Each hero should face a personalized phantom — tailored to their backstory, their guilt, their fears.

THE TRAP: The phantoms are designed to provoke violence. If a hero gives in to fear and attacks the phantom with lethal intent, they are firing blindly at their own friends in the dark. The illusions make allies look like enemies.

THE WHISPERS: The phantoms whisper that the Vel Shara fortune will never wash the blood from their hands. They know about the Ewoks. About Denia. About every compromise the crew has made.

THE WAY OUT: To break the illusion, the heroes must see through the paranoia, lower their weapons, and trust their allies over their own lying eyes. They must accept the ugly truth of their pasts to shatter the mirrors and reunite.

SUCCESS: Heroes who overcome the phantoms gain clarity — a sense of peace, however brief, in the heart of the dark. They see through the Nexus's tricks for the remainder of the Spire.

FAILURE: Heroes who attack the phantoms wound their allies and carry the psychological damage forward. The dark got inside their heads, and it doesn't fully let go.

npcs.name: Dark Side Phantoms
npcs.notes: Personalized to each hero. Not physically real — projections of the Dark Side Nexus weaponizing memory and guilt.
npcs.behavior: Speak with impossible voices. Know details only the hero would know. Designed to provoke lethal violence — the hero attacks the phantom and hits an ally instead.
dialogue: "You left them to die. You always leave them to die."
"The fortune at Vel Shara won't wash the blood from your hands."
"You're no different from the Inquisitor. You just tell yourself a better story."
npcs.intel: The phantoms are not real. They are the Nexus testing whether the heroes can distinguish fear from truth. The only way out is trust.

decisions.choice: Conscience over Violence — Lower weapons, trust allies, accept the ugly truths to shatter the illusions
decisions.consequence: The mirrors break. The crew reunites. Heroes who overcome the phantoms gain a moment of clarity in the heart of the dark.

decisions.choice: Give in to fear — Attack the phantoms with lethal intent
decisions.consequence: The heroes fire blindly at their own friends in the dark. Allies are wounded. The psychological damage carries forward — the dark got inside their heads.

narrativeLinks.note: The mirrors shatter. The crew reunites and presses deeper toward the meditation chamber.

pacing.openingBeat: The cold. The frost. The creeping isolation.
pacing.risingAction: Separation. Each hero faces their phantom alone.
pacing.climax: The moment of choice — trust or violence. Lower the weapon or pull the trigger.
pacing.resolution: The mirrors shatter. The crew reunites, shaken but whole (or wounded by friendly fire).

scenes.title: The Hollowed
scenes.subtitle: The Bound Spirits
scenes.readAloud:
Just before the cavern opens, the crew enters a shattered meditation antechamber. The walls are lined with the mummified, skeletal husks of ancient dark side acolytes who came seeking Revan's power centuries ago.

As the heroes step into the chamber, the temperature drops so rapidly that frost blooms on their armor. A sickly, violent light — reminiscent of Dathomiri spirit ichor, but corrupted and violet — sparks deep within the empty eye sockets of the corpses.

The husks begin to move. Joints pop. Ancient dust cascades from their robes as the tortured spirits forcibly reanimate their own ruined bodies. They whisper in a hundred overlapping, echoing voices, filled with centuries of trapped madness. They ignite ancient, unstable lightsabers that sputter and crackle, blocking the path forward.
scenes.gmNotes:
This is the Nightsister's spotlight scene. Her unique knowledge of Dathomiri spirit-binding magick makes her the fulcrum of this encounter.

THE SPIRITS: These are not zombies — they are tortured souls trapped in their own corpses for centuries by the Dark Side Nexus. They don't want to fight. They are compelled to. Their whispers are madness and pain, not aggression.

THE NIGHTSISTER'S CHOICE: She recognizes this immediately as a crude, agonizing perversion of spirit-binding magicks. She has two paths:

DOMINATION: She opens her mind to the void and attempts to violently dominate the spirits, bending them to her will to clear the path. This works — but it means linking her mind directly to centuries of concentrated dark side insanity. An intoxicating, dangerous shortcut to power. This is using the dead as tools — exactly what Varth does to the living.

RELEASE: A grueling, close-quarters battle to physically shatter the husks, severing the spirits' anchor to the physical world and granting them the violent release they have been denied for centuries. Harder, costlier, but merciful.

VARTH'S ABSENCE: For the first time, the heroes face a horrifying threat without Varth's tactical brilliance in their ears telling them to take the easy, ruthless way out. The choice of what kind of people they are rests entirely on their own shoulders.

npcs.name: The Hollowed
npcs.notes: Ancient corpses reanimated by trapped, tortured spirits. Unstable lightsabers. Centuries of madness.
npcs.behavior: Compelled to fight but not willingly aggressive. Whisper fragmented pleas between attacks. The spirits are trapped, not malevolent — the Nexus forces them to guard the path.
dialogue: "Free us... please... we cannot stop..."
"The dark... it holds us... it won't let go..."
"We came for power... we found only chains..."
npcs.intel: The spirits can be dominated (Nightsister magick) or released (physical destruction of the husks). Domination is faster but carries a dark side cost. Release is harder but merciful.

decisions.choice: Dominion — The Nightsister opens her mind to the void and dominates the trapped spirits
decisions.consequence: The spirits are enslaved and the path clears. The Nightsister gains a dangerous taste of dark side power — intoxicating and corrosive. She used the dead as tools, just as Varth uses the living.

decisions.choice: Release — The crew fights to physically shatter the husks and free the trapped spirits
decisions.consequence: A brutal, close-quarters battle. The crew takes injuries but grants the tortured souls the release they've been denied for centuries. Merciful, costly, and right.

narrativeLinks.note: The meditation chamber clears. The path opens to the Abyssal Vault.

pacing.openingBeat: The temperature drops. The husks begin to glow. Ancient lightsabers ignite.
pacing.risingAction: The spirits whisper. The Nightsister recognizes the magick. Two paths emerge.
pacing.climax: Domination or release. The choice that defines the Nightsister's arc.
pacing.resolution: The path clears. The cost is paid, one way or another.

scenes.title: The Abyssal Vault
scenes.subtitle: The Dragon's Awakening
scenes.readAloud:
The claustrophobic corridors finally break open into a colossal, natural subterranean cavern. Massive, gnarly petrified roots, as thick as dreadnoughts, weave an interconnected web across a staggering expanse. Far below, lost in the gloom, rests a pool of dark, perfectly still water. Spanning the abyss is a heavy, ancient stone bridge leading to a massive, circular Vault Door that Draco has already blown open.

At the center of the platform stands Inquisitor Valin Draco. He holds the fragmented halves of Revan's soul: the azure Jedi Holocron in one hand, the crimson Sith Holocron in the other. Imperial Dark Troopers are positioned throughout the tangled roots above, locking their heavy repeating blasters onto the heroes. They have the high ground and a lethal angle of fire.

Draco looks at the heroes with aristocratic amusement. With a casual, insulting flick of his wrist, he tosses the Jedi Holocron. It clatters across the stone bridge, stopping near the heroes' boots.
scenes.gmNotes:
This is the climactic set-piece of the adventure. Draco, the Dark Troopers, the Jedi and Sith Holocrons, and the Dark Side Dragon — everything converges here.

DRACO'S PLAY: He tosses the Jedi Holocron as bait. He doesn't need it — the Sith Holocron is his prize. Throwing the Jedi Holocron at the heroes is an insult: 'Take your scraps and run.' He expects them to grab it and flee while he claims the real power.

THE SITH HOLOCRON: Draco places it on an ancient pedestal at the vault's threshold. It pulses with blinding red light, imbuing him with raw dark side energy. But the Nexus demands a toll.

THE DRAGON'S AWAKENING: The dark, still water far below violently churns. Subtly influenced by Draco's dominating will, a colossal Dark Side Dragon — a massive, blind, serpentine horror of pale scales and needle-teeth — slithers rapidly up the petrified roots. It turns the cavern into utter chaos, snapping blindly at the bridge and the roots.

THE HALF-MEASURE: The safe play is to grab the Jedi Holocron and run. Half the map, survival guaranteed. The dangerous play is to push through the Dark Troopers and the Dragon to tear the Sith Holocron from the pedestal — risking annihilation for the complete set.

THE REJECTION: The Sith Holocron recognizes Draco's weakness. He is an Imperial attack dog, not a true inheritor of Revan's power. The Holocron violently rejects him — an arc of crimson lightning strikes him in the chest, hurling him off the platform into the abyss below. He survives (escaping through underground tunnels) but the heroes don't know that.

DRACO'S SURVIVAL: He escapes through the reservoir tunnels, barely avoiding the Dragon. His injuries are massive — this is the origin of his cybernetic additions when the heroes encounter him again on Eriadu in Adventure 6. They will not hear from him between now and then.

npcs.name: Inquisitor Valin Draco
npcs.notes: The Empire's blade. Aristocratic, contemptuous, supremely confident. Holds both holocrons when the heroes arrive.
npcs.behavior: Theatrical contempt. Tosses the Jedi Holocron as an insult. Places the Sith Holocron on the pedestal to absorb its power. His arrogance is his undoing — the Holocron rejects him violently.
dialogue: "Ah. Varth's errand runners. I was wondering when you'd stumble down here."
"Take it. Take the Jedi's trinket. Run back to your admiral and tell him the Empire sends its regards."
"This... this power... it's mine. MINE—"
npcs.intel: Draco is ejected by the Sith Holocron and falls into the abyss. He survives through the underground tunnels but sustains massive injuries — origin of his cybernetics in Adventure 6 on Eriadu.

npcs.name: Imperial Dark Troopers
npcs.notes: Positioned in the petrified roots above. Heavy repeating blasters. Superior positioning.
npcs.behavior: Disciplined fire. Hold the high ground. Maintain crossfire on the bridge. When the Dragon emerges, some panic and break formation.
npcs.intel: Dark Troopers — heavier and better equipped than standard Stormtroopers. Their discipline breaks when the Dragon appears.

npcs.name: Dark Side Dragon
npcs.notes: A massive, blind, serpentine creature of pale scales and needle-teeth. Awakened by the Sith Holocron's activation. Lives in the subterranean reservoir beneath the vault.
npcs.behavior: Blind. Hunts by vibration and Dark Side resonance. Snaps at the bridge, the roots, anything that moves. Not malicious — just ancient, territorial, and enormous. Turns the entire cavern into a multi-level chaos zone.
npcs.intel: The Dragon is a product of millennia of Dark Side corruption — the ecosystem of the Nexus given terrible physical form. It is not killable in any practical sense. It is an environmental hazard.

decisions.choice: The Half-Measure — Snatch the Jedi Holocron and run, securing half the map and prioritizing survival
decisions.consequence: The crew escapes with the Jedi Holocron (incomplete navigation data). Safer, but they leave the Sith Holocron for the Empire to potentially recover. The charts will need drift algorithms to be usable regardless.

decisions.choice: The Grave — Push forward into the teeth of the Dark Troopers and the Dragon to seize the Sith Holocron from the pedestal
decisions.consequence: The crew risks annihilation but may secure both holocrons — the complete set of Revan's navigation data. Maximum risk, maximum reward. The Sith Holocron rejects Draco during the assault, creating a window of chaos.

narrativeLinks.note: Draco is ejected into the abyss. The Spire destabilizes. The crew must flee.

pacing.openingBeat: The cavern opens. Draco stands on the platform with both holocrons. Dark Troopers lock on.
pacing.risingAction: Draco tosses the Jedi Holocron. The insult. The choice crystallizes.
pacing.climax: The Dragon awakens. The Sith Holocron rejects Draco. Total chaos.
pacing.resolution: Draco falls into the abyss. The Spire screams. The crew grabs what they can.

scenes.title: The Fall of the Black Tree
scenes.subtitle: Maya's Extraction
scenes.readAloud:
The cavern violently shakes as the dark side energy destabilizes. The structural integrity of the petrified roots begins to fail. The crew snatches whatever holocrons remain and sprints back up the spiraling stairs, lungs burning as Revan's ancient masonry cracks and groans, collapsing into the abyss behind them.

As they burst through the heavy stone door, tumbling out into the gray dust of the withered clearing, the Endor twilight hits their eyes. The oppressive jamming of the Nexus lifts. The comm-link violently cracks to life in a burst of frantic static.

"—Ace! Boss! Do you read me?!"

It is Varth. But the smug, charming employer is gone. He is shouting, his voice tight with genuine panic over the roar of distant engines.

"I have massive Imperial signatures dropping right on top of you! They just burned a vertical corridor through the canopy two kilometers east of your position! You have minutes before that entire tree comes down! Maya is already in the air — she's coming to you!"

The impossibly dense canopy of the dead zone has been violently sheared open. Smoke billows from shattered, petrified branches where Imperial gunships blasted a drop-zone. The whine of approaching troop transports fills the air, and white-armored Stormtroopers are already breaking through the tree line, advancing on the clearing.
scenes.readAloudPart2:
Suddenly, the deafening roar of sublight engines drowns out the Imperial sirens.

Maya did not wait for an all-clear. Pushing the Banshee far beyond its structural tolerances, she dives straight down the smoking, narrow drop-corridor the Imperials just carved. The hull shrieks as it scrapes against jagged timber. She is playing chicken with Imperial airspace, risking her ship — her lifeblood — to get her crew out.

The ground violently heaves. The Ebon Spire is screaming, deep fissures racing up its black, obsidian-like trunk. Maya brings the Banshee into a violent hover just meters above the gray dust, the repulsorlifts kicking up a blinding storm. The boarding ramp drops open mid-air.

"JUMP!" Maya screams over the external loudspeakers.

The heroes make a desperate, scrambling sprint through the crossfire, diving onto the open ramp. The second their boots hit the deck, Maya throws the yoke hard, tearing back up toward the canopy.

Beneath them, the Ebon Spire finally gives way. Millennia of petrified, dark-side-infused wood shatters with a sound like a dying god. The colossal black tree collapses inward, sending a shockwave of dust tearing across the clearing, crushing the advancing Imperial cordons under the ruin of Revan's legacy.
scenes.readAloudPart1PauseNote: Let the panic of the Imperial arrival and Varth's genuine fear land. Then hit them with Maya's dive.
scenes.gmNotes:
This is the action climax and the emotional payoff. Everything the adventure built — the isolation, the moral choices, the horror of the Spire — resolves in a desperate sprint and Maya's reckless, loyal extraction.

VARTH'S PANIC: This is the first time the crew has heard genuine fear in Varth's voice. He is not performing — the Imperial signatures are real and unexpected. His investment (the crew + the holocrons) is about to be destroyed. The panic is real, but its motivation is selfish.

MAYA'S MOMENT: She does not wait for permission. She flies the Banshee down a corridor the Empire just blasted through the canopy — reckless, loyal, and exactly the kind of thing that makes players love her. This is her 'I am the pilot' moment. She risks her ship — her lifeblood — to get her people out.

THE COLLAPSE: The Ebon Spire falls. Millennia of dark-side-infused wood shatters. The Nexus is broken. The clearing is buried. The Imperial advance is crushed under the ruin. This is final — the Spire cannot be revisited.

VARTH IN THE CARGO HOLD: As the Banshee breaks atmosphere, Varth meets the crew in the cargo hold. He looks shaken, relieved — a man who just barely pulled his people out of the fire. To the bleeding, exhausted heroes, he is their savior. He saw the Empire coming and sent the cavalry. The illusion of his loyalty is perfectly cemented in blood and adrenaline.

The trap is set for Adventure 5 and the betrayal in Adventure 6.

npcs.name: Maya
npcs.notes: Flies the Banshee down the Imperial drop-corridor without waiting for an all-clear. Reckless, brilliant, loyal.
npcs.behavior: Pure action. No hesitation. She risks the Banshee — her most precious possession — to extract her crew. Screaming orders over the loudspeakers. This is her at her absolute best.
dialogue: "JUMP!"
"Hold on to something — this isn't going to be pretty!"
npcs.intel: Maya's loyalty is genuine and unconditional. She is the moral counterweight to Varth. Everything she does here is real.

npcs.name: Admiral Varth
npcs.notes: Genuinely panicked on comms — the Imperial signatures are unexpected and threaten his investment. Meets the crew in the cargo hold afterward, shaken and relieved.
npcs.behavior: First genuine fear the crew has heard from him. Then, in the cargo hold, quiet relief. He is their savior — he spotted the Empire and sent Maya. The illusion of loyalty is cemented.
dialogue: "—Ace! Boss! Do you read me?!"
"I have massive Imperial signatures dropping right on top of you!"
"Maya is already in the air — she's coming to you!"
"You made it. You actually made it. I... good. That's good. Let's get off this rock."
npcs.intel: Varth's panic is real but selfishly motivated — the crew and the holocrons are his investment. His relief in the cargo hold cements the illusion of loyalty heading into Adventures 5 and 6.

npcs.name: Imperial Stormtroopers
npcs.notes: Advancing from the drop-corridor through the tree line. Standard equipment. Overwhelmed by the Spire's collapse.
npcs.behavior: Advancing in formation. Firing on the crew during the sprint to the Banshee. Crushed by the collapsing Spire.
npcs.intel: Imperial response force. Their presence confirms that Draco called for backup — the Empire knew the heroes were here.

pacing.openingBeat: Comms crack to life. Varth is panicking. Imperial signatures everywhere.
pacing.risingAction: Stormtroopers advancing. The Spire is dying. No extraction in sight.
pacing.climax: Maya's dive. The Banshee screaming down the drop-corridor. 'JUMP!'
pacing.resolution: The Spire falls. The Banshee breaks atmosphere. Varth meets them in the cargo hold — their savior.
